danger_signals.visual_form: Colored bullets fired from enemy positions
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring evasion; player takes damage on contact

danger_signals.visual_form: Large distinctive enemy sprite at stage conclusion
danger_signals.meaning: Stage climax; heightened danger requiring pattern recognition

danger_signals.visual_form: Waves of smaller enemies advancing toward player
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating pressure; coordinated threat requiring tactical repositioning
Collision damage; terrain can eliminate all remaining lives if player gets squeezed (JP version mechanic)

danger_signals.visual_form: Character death animation (disintegration in JP, blue ball explosion in US/EU)
danger_signals.meaning: Life loss; visual distinction between versions emphasizes severity

safety_signals.meaning: Weapon upgrades (Laser, Wide Beam, Bomb) or support items (speed boost, life recovery)

safety_signals.visual_form: Open flight corridors between enemy clusters
safety_signals.meaning: Safe navigation routes; designed evasion paths

safety_signals.visual_form: Two orbiting defensive units above and below player
safety_signals.meaning: Increased firepower and coverage; protective formation

safety_signals.visual_form: Pre-stage screen showing level layout, score, lives, future stages (US/EU versions)
safety_signals.meaning: Contextual safety briefing; player orientation and resource awareness

faction_indicators.faction: SCAT (Special Cybernetic Attack Team) / SAT (European version)
faction_indicators.color_identity: Red and blue palette-swapped characters (Arnold/male soldier vs Sigourney/female soldier or Silver Man/Sparks in EU)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character designs as part-human part-machine soldiers
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: No visual faction variants; enemy types are universal alien/mechanical designs
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Futuristic military aesthetic in stage progression (urban ruins, battleship, astrotube station)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Diverse enemy sprite designs representing extraterrestrial/mechanical origin
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Various enemy types with different movement patterns and projectile styles per stage
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Astrotube structure (space station connection); sci-fi technology aesthetic; stage 5 astrotube, stage 4 battleship settings

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Alien invasion active; Earth under attack (year 2029)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Urban decay environments; ruins of New York City as opening stages; progression toward alien space station
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy waves escalate; bosses represent command hierarchy of invasion force

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: SCAT team characters in combat armor; two-player simultaneous action emphasizing team coordination
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Players control cybernetic soldiers with dual-satellite weapon systems; demonstrates advanced human-machine integration

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map screen (US/EU) shows future stages; each completed stage visually transitions environment from urban to military structures to alien installations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss encounters mark territorial control points; defeating bosses unlocks access to next invasion stage

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Players start with 3 hit points vs 6 in US/EU; weapon loss on any single hit vs only on game over
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Satellite behavior differs: auto-orbiting vs player-directed alignment; terrain collision causes total life loss vs single life loss

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: US/EU versions feature character select screen for 1-player mode (Arnold/Sigourney or Silver Man/Sparks); Russian-coded character removed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Gentler difficulty curve; extended resource pool encourages progression over precision

environmental_storytelling.example: Stages transition from New York ruins → battleship arena → astrotube space station, visually narrating the invasion route and player's counter-invasion path
Japanese version shows obliteration of cities before invasion begins (apocalyptic framing); US/EU versions show text-based briefing (tactical framing)

Part-human, part-machine soldier designs (visible in sprite work) communicate cybernetic enhancement theme; different regional names (Arnold/Sigourney vs Silver Man/Sparks) reflect localization but maintain visual character consistency
Different enemy sprites per stage represent alien hierarchy and technological progression; bosses are visually distinct, larger entities suggesting command authority
Satellite module positioning (orbiting vs static) and firepower differences between versions communicate escalation of player capability relative to enemy waves

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Eight-directional movement; continuous screen scroll forces forward momentum; weapon fires left/right only
Character disintegrates animation; loses equipped weapon; loses one life; pushed along by scrolling screen if terrain-squeezed
Character explodes into blue balls (Mega Man-style); loses weapon only on game over, not single hit; terrain squeeze causes immediate game over
Auto-orbit above/below player; fire in sync with player weapon; bullets are uncolored
Align based on player horizontal movement; can be locked in position via A button; bullets match player color; positioned more responsively to player input
Coordinated patterns requiring tactical evasion; intensity scales within stage
Large sprite; distinctive movement and attack patterns; multiple hit requirement; incomplete graphics set exists (Level 3 boss), suggesting design iteration
Visible in environment; require destruction to activate; reward risk-taking by player

notes: S.C.A.T. demonstrates sophisticated version localization affecting both mechanical and narrative manifestation layers. The Japanese 'Final Mission' uses visual feedback (disintegration death, weapon loss on hit, auto-orbiting satellites) to communicate higher world-state threat. US/EU 'S.C.A.T.' and EU 'Action in New York' soften this through explosion death, retained weapons on hit, and player-controlled satellite alignment. The astrotube setting (alien space station connection) and progression from urban ruins through battleship to space installation creates environmental narrative of invasion. Character customization (male/female vs palette-swapped soldiers) was localized for market reception. Faction distinction relies on visual contrast: human-machine cybernetic soldiers vs diverse alien enemy types, with boss encounters marking territorial control points. Pre-stage maps (US/EU only) provide world-state context absent in Japanese version.
